displayTitle: 2025-12-11 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Ross Hoole: Require an independent review of decisions by the Judicial Conduct Commissioner
topic: Petition of Ross Hoole: Require an independent review of decisions by the Judicial Conduct Commissioner
description: Petition of Ross Hoole: Require an independent review of decisions by the Judicial Conduct Commissioner Ross Hoole
descriptionSummary: Petition of Ross Hoole: Require an independent review of decisions by the Judicial Conduct Commissioner. Ross Hoole

chunks.text: Kia ora. Good afternoon. Kia ora. Kahurangi Carter, list MP for the Greens based in Christchurch. Fantastic. Good afternoon. All right. Okay. Thanks, Mr. Hall. So, um, we've got, uh, 10 minutes. You can take your, um, papers, your very informative and well-researched and well-prepared papers as, as read. However, no doubt you'll, uh, take us through.

Just a reminder with, uh, select committees, obviously, um, criticism of judges, um, is something that, uh, is... is... goes against our standing orders here. So just a rem- But I'm sure I don't need to remind you of that. It was the respectable... very respectful submission today. So thank you.

Okay. Thank you very much. Okay. I'll start. Um, thank you for the opportunity to make this oral submission to the, um, Petitions Committee. The purpose of the Judicial Conduct Commission and Judicial Conduct Panel Act is for a robust investigation process, section 4A. Five pertinent sections are found in that act, which make up the body of the commissioner's actions and decisions.

Withdrawal of complaints, no further action, section 15A, dismissed, section 16, referred to head of bench, section 17, section 8 (1) (c) and section 8B, and then recommendation for judicial conduct panel to be established, section 18. In the 2024/'25 annual report, of the 403 complaints finalized, 104 had no further action taken, 277 dismissed outright, seven referred to heads of benches, and four are recommended for a judicial conduct panel to be

established. Thus, the majority of complaints, 381 out of 403 complaints, 97.2%, were not even mentioned in the report, other than a figure in the table. These are the highest figures in the last six years of complaints being disregarded and no information provided to justify this occurring. Complaints for people other than judges. The annual reports over the years do not say how many complaints received by the commissioner were for people other than judges. Those non-judicial complaints do not fall under the jurisdiction of the act in

terms of section 16A... 1A. If clearly identified, this may have hopefully reduced the number of complaints reflected as being against judges in the annual reports. Complaints where no further action is taken, section 15A, in the current 2020... '24/'25 report, 104 complaints, 25.8% of all complaints fall into this category.

And the public have no idea if the discretion was correct or not, as there is just silence. Complaints which were dismissed, section 16, in the current '24/'25 report, 277 complaints, 68.7% of all complaints fall under this category. And no one questions why no fir- no information is provided in the report, the most significant section of complaints, and the report is blank. It is this section where the commissioner's interpretation of the word jurisdiction in section 16 (1) (a)

contradicts section 16 (1) (b) . Sections 16 (1) (a) - (i) need to be unpacked, showing how many complaints were dismissed in terms of each subsection. Section 16 (1) says the commissioner must dismiss the complaint if the commissioner is of the opinion that, A, the complaint is not within the commissioner's jurisdiction.

A clear understanding of the term jurisdiction in the act can be defined... Remember, it's can, mine, um, as the jurisdiction of the Judicial Conduct Commissioner and Judicial Conduct Panel Act 20- 2004 is all judges and justices within New Zealand and the Chatham Islands. So that is my definition, um, because there is no definition th- in the act and that is a problem. Um, section 16 (1) (b) says that complaint has no bearing on judicial functions or judicial duties. This means all complaints with

bearing on judicial functions or judicial duties are valid complaints. There is no information in the report to indicate that section 16 (1) (b) has been followed. It would appear that the commissioner has actually been applying the direct opposite under section 16 (1) (a) . Another pertinent section is, um, section 16 (1) (f) , which says the commissioner must dismiss a complaint if the complaint is about a judicial decision or other judicial function that is or was subject to a right of appeal or right to apply for judicial review.

The only way that these complaints can be dismissed is if there is a right of appeal, meaning a person has further legal channels to follow. Sadly, only the defendant, and thus defense counsel and prosecutor, have a right to appeal. A witness or victim in a criminal case does not have a right to appeal. And thus, section 16 (1) (f) only directly applies to the defendant, defense counsel, and the prosecutor. Everyone else is subject to the prosecutor agreeing to lodge an appeal.

And I'm very much aware of that because I sit in that situation. A question needs to be asked-... would a prosecutor lodge an appeal when it could bring into question the prosecutor's own work in the case, or lack thereof? Also, how can a person appeal a judge's decision when the judge has broken a law while performing his duties?

As no appeal action can unbreak a law and the damage is done to the person and cannot be undone. Complaints which refer to heads of benches. This is my positive point. I think the '24/'25 annual report gave clear details of all seven complaints referred to the relevant head of bench. This section is well-presented, pointing out the inappropriate conduct but these judges...

by these judges which require correction. Complaints which recommend the attorney general to appoint a Judicial Conduct Panel. In the '24/'25 annual report, the table reflects that four complaints were referred to the attorney general, but the report only mentions one referral.

This means that the attorney general, who's required to check this report, has not done her judiciary duties and allowed the report to go to the House of Representatives with this clear error. Either the statistics are incorrect or there were three other referrals to the attorney general which are not mentioned in the report. The one complaint referred to the attorney general has been in the newspapers.

Wha- what about the other three referrals, showing something is actually going wrong here? Um, the weak argument used by the Judicial Conduct Commissioner to justify how he does his work. In the '24/'25 annual report, once again, the commissioner interprets Section 16 (1) (a) by turning to Section 8 (2) . But Section 8 (2) talks about function and not jurisdiction.

The commiss- commissioner's jurisdiction is to deal with judges only. The Judicial Conduct Panel is to investigate. Section 26 (1) states the Judicial Conduct Panel has the same powers as are conferred on commissions of inquiry by Sections 4 and 4 (b) to (8) of the Commissions of Inquiry Act 1908. Section 11, titled "Which complaints must be dealt with," unpacks what the commissioner must deal with, clearly showing his function and jurisdiction. Section 11 (1) states, "The commissioner must receive and deal with every complaint made

under this section about the conduct of a judge, regardless of whether the subject matter of the complaint arises in the exercise of the judge's judicial duties or otherwise." The commissioner is required to e- even consider if the action was a criminal offense, as stated in Section 11 (2) . An alternative route to resolve issues. How can a country run a dual system, one in terms of legislation and one informal to deal with complaints against judges? If anyone else did this, it would be deemed as vigilantism. The reason for this informal

system being required needs to be investigated as the legal fraternity feel thwarted by the commissioner to deal with their complaints addressed via the legal route. What is currently happening, minor complaints are being dismissed by the commissioner while similar complaints are being dealt with by the Law Society to reach the heads of benches and bypassing the commissioner and the Act. It is either in or out. You cannot ha- have it both ways. The wording of the 2024/'25 report shows that the commissioner realizes

that there are exceptions to his interpretation of Section 16 (1) (a) , (8) (ii) and 16 (1) (f) ) , but he is still silent on Section 16 (1) (b) ) . Thus, the commissioner has admitted in writing to Parliament that he has been dismissing complaints which could have been referred for further action but declined to because of his interpretation of jurisdiction. The only way to unravel what has been happening within the work of the commission is to appoint an independent body to review each decision of all Judicial Conduct Commissioners,

starting with the current reporting period and systematically working back to the first report. Only then will the rep- the public know and understand what is currently shrouded in mystery, and judges will be held to account for their actions. Thank you very much. Thank you, Mr. Hall. Would you imagine that complaints would go directly to this new panel that you're proposing, um, or...

Yeah. ... or what would, what would be the, what would be the threshold? No, what I'm proposing is that we actually have a, a panel to actually go and look to see if the decisions where the... and especially Section, Section 16 (1) (a) ) , where the commissioner currently is dismissing complaints what, for what is actually said to be jurisdiction, but he's interpreting as function. And we need to look at those decisions and see if they were actually valid decisions.

And until we actually divide from what is a complaint against a judge and what is a complaint against a officer of the court, a prosecutor, whatever else, but an oth- a non-judge person, then only... 'Cause that is the valid reason why Section 16 (1) (a) ) is, um, being asked. So, until we split those two, then we'll have this body of people where currently the commissioner is-... saying it's not my function to deal with it because it's actually going into details of what a judge has actually made a decision.

But that's why I'm saying that Section 16 (1) (b) is not being considered, because Section 1C (b) is, it's the opposite of (b) which needs to be considered by the commissioner. So once we've dealt with non-judge things, now we've got the ones which are being dismissed because of jurisdiction, and those are not jurisdiction. Those are the very things.

That's why I'm saying that the panel is the one which has got the powers and needs to do the investigations, not the commissioner. That the commissioner currently is dismissing complaints because he's saying, "That was a decision made in a court, in a judgment, and I'm not allowed to touch it." But clearly that isn't in contradiction of Section 16 (1) (b) , which is actually saying the opposite to that, um, because it's saying not a judicial function or judicial duty. Um, so- Um, uh, okay. Vanessa Wenning?

Um, thank you very much. Oh, we're just, we are a little time constrained, Mr. Paul. Yeah. Just, um, what's, what do you think the volume of the work that's needed here would be? Just have understanding. Okay. We're talking about, like, for this financial year- Yeah.... ending, um, 31st of July was 277, which he, um, which were dismissed, um, outright. They just need to be going and recategorized. Ones which were for non-judges, and then looking at those and saying, "Which ones could have actually been considered?" 'Cause clearly the commissioner in this latest report is admitting

that he will, under certain discretion, actually allow things to go through, but he's not allowing all to go through. So my opinion is there might be... I mean, I have no clue because I don't know how many were for non-judge people, and that actually is the category. 'Cause now you're talking about 403, but that 403 actually might only be 300. And so suddenly... So there might be 150, 200 applications which need to be looked at, or 200 complaints looked at, and to say, "Were they jurisdiction?" No. And were they, yes, they are jurisdiction.

We've got to then recategorize them and look under the other categories, including Section 16 (1) (f) , which is, could the person appeal? I see. But that means more complaints will go through to the heads of benches for them to address, and there might be a couple of hundred going through every year, going through to the heads of benches, and not just like the seven.

Sadly, the Law Society does not keep records. They destroy their records soon as they've actually gone to the heads of benches. So one's not actually able to understand how many complaints are going from the legal fraternity through the Law Society to, um, heads of benches. But clearly there must be quite a few.

And I'm not taking any- All right. So, look, no, so, Mr. ... Sorry, Mr. Paul, we, we do have limited time. Yeah, sorry. You're making very long answers. Um, we've got time for one more question. Sorry. We've, uh, this is why I do encourage, um, submitters to w- we, we have read the submission, but, Actually, Mr. Chair, I think he's answered my question. All right. Uh, you have, uh... Okay.

Well, just give a chance then, just to give us a quick summary, um, and then I'll, uh, we will, um, the, the, the, uh, panel will now retire to consider. So just, anything just sum up your position, perhaps you'd like to... Okay. I feel that removing non-judges, suddenly w- the country will say there's not actually so many bad judges. I think if things go through the heads of benches, um, complainants will be much happier that something has actually happened, and maybe we will see judges actually being, um, more circumspect in what actually they say and

don't, um, shall we say, lose their rag or, or say things, and that they might actually apply the law much more clearly. Um, thank you. All right. Look, well, thank you. Um, very, very full, um, submission. So, uh, what we'll do now is that, uh, obviously we, uh, um, are receiving some, um, submissions from other organizations who are involved in the, in the, um, in, in the process.
So, and then we'll report back to Parliament on that. So thank you very much. Uh, much appreciated. Thank you. Thank you. Much ap- Thank you very much.
